author: @locouk
text:
I try and reclaim any lithium cell be it an 18650 to a 250mAh tiny cell. 
  Some of the single use e-cigs carried a rechargeable lithium cell, I think Big Clive has covered it a while ago.

author: @Teknopottu
text: Sorry to say but this was one of the less usefull DIY-battery-videos. Even plain dangerous. It's great to encourage someone to do something yourself but there is truly good (and safe) ways to disassemble battery packs and use the cells. You talked almost nothing about safety precautions when dealing something with a punch like lithium ion cells and testing of them to make out which are usefull and which could burn down your house after couple of cycles or just die out. Not even anything about figuring out the actual capacity of the cells. This is the first video I've disliked from you guys but with a reason. There is always a risk when dealing with DIY-batteries but it's foolish to gamble before studying even the basic risks. Youtube offers lots of videos on safe disassembling without puncturing cells, proper testing on capacity, resistance etc. and putting together with battery management systems. There is no shame in learning before showing how.

author: @robtitheridge9708
text: i have been useing recyled laptop battries for a long time  its a good idea. i do recomend a BMS i have had one flat pack explode and an 18650 catch fire both times ehen charging.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: UH OH, That sounds like fun lol I hope everything is okay! I agree a BMS is very important, I think I will order 2 just in case. I don't want this to happen. Thanks for the heads up mate! :)

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Glad you mentioned BMS systems they are a must have when charging lithium packs.. Also due to the range of voltages between full and flat (16.8v - 12v) it is always best to use a 12v regulator when you're wanting a constant 12v. They are very easy to make, an LM7812, a couple of capacitors and a heat sink. 
By the way excellent video, pity you only got 50% of the cells being good.

author: @milliosmiles5160
text: Without the management system the 18650s run the risk of creating a shunt and destroying the end battery.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Nice. Wise to use the tco.  18650 can do 50 amps when shorted. 6 x 4 x 3.6 x 50 = 4320 W. However 3.6 will drop at that current, lts say it'll be 2000W. It would be up in smoke in minutes.
A former collegue of mine actually made an electric bike with salvaged laptop batteries. It sure can be usefull.

You can do CPR on some dead batteries by connecting their terminals to another 18650 live battery - positive to positive and negative to negative for about 15 seconds. 

Try it!

author: @infinitelyuniversal2390
text:
Hi Luke,
I build batteries as hobby, and when you salvage used or even build with brand new cells, it is Extremely important to test internal resistance, and actual capacity of each cell, then you must arrange them with similar values, on top of that should install BMS BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM OR BATTERY MONITORING SYSTEM, to avoid cell failure and potential fires 🔥,  safety first☆ this is very common people burning there homes down!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thank you for the heads up mate! I agree a BMS is definitely important, I think I will order 2 just to have on the battery packs. Cheers mate! :)

5. Can you charge it?

replies.author: @audriusradziunas5451

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text:
You'll still want to get a BMS. They're pretty cheap on Banggood or Aliexpress. If you have one cell that's a bit worse than the others in your series string, it'll reach 0V before the rest, and you'll be reverse charging the cell. That won't likely cause a fire, but will nuke the capacity.
It's probably also a good idea to 'bin' the cells in a series string to match cells with similar capacities for an overall bigger capacity battery.

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: Built a portable 13Ah battery pack from jerrycan and 18650s from powertools couple of years ago. Added bluetooth amplifier, car speakers, cigaret lighter plug, 5V USB-outputs and hand crank generator made out of a windshield wiper motor and a crank from an old meat grinder. Took months to gather stuff and order the rest from ebay but it definately was worth the effort because i made something unique and usefull myself with fraction of the price of something even close to my dear abomination's capabilities :)
